task: I’m trying to tighten up how I run a small rehab from offer to close, and I want a simple checklist that catches weak spots before they turn into delays. What are the few things you’d put in place so vendors, inspections, and paperwork stay aligned without me babysitting every step?

turns.agent_response:
Use a **tight control stack** with four things: **scope, schedule, docs, and hold points**.

## 1) One master scope sheet
Have a single document that lists:
- every room / area
- every trade
- every material allowance
- every approved change order
- every exclusion

If it’s not on the sheet, it doesn’t exist.

## 2) One schedule with dependencies
Build the job around:
- demo
- roughs
- inspections
- drywall
- finish work
- final punch
- closeout

Mark:
- what must finish before the next trade starts
- what can overlap
- what is a long-lead item

This keeps vendors from freelancing out of sequence.

## 3) One document log
Track:
- permits
- invoices
- lien waivers
- inspection reports
- photos
- warranty docs
- change orders
- payoff / closing docs if relevant

No document, no assumption.

## 4) Hold points before money moves
Tie payment to:
- scope completion
- inspection pass
- photo confirmation
- paperwork receipt
- lien waivers
- final walkthrough

This is how you stop partial completion from getting paid like final completion.

## 5) Weekly control check
Once a week, confirm:
- what is done
- what is blocked
- what is late
- what is waiting on inspection
- what is waiting on material
- what is waiting on paperwork

That tells you where the job is slipping before it becomes expensive.

## 6) One point of contact per lane
Don’t let three people answer for the same trade.
- one vendor contact
- one inspection contact
- one lender/closing contact
- one owner decision point

That prevents mixed signals.

## Simple rule
If a task affects **scope, schedule, inspection, or payment**, it gets written down and tracked.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page rehab control checklist** you can use from offer to close.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. A little generic though. I’d want the actual checklist, not the theory.

What I’d add is: pre-close verification on title, permit status, utility shutoff/restart, and any open code issues. That’s where the dumb delays come from. Also a hard rule on change orders — no verbal approvals, ever. If it changes scope or finish date, it gets written, priced, and tied to a revised schedule before anyone touches it.

And I’d want one person owning the log, not a committee. Too many hands and things drift. If you can, make the checklist by phase: offer/due diligence, pre-close, first 30 days, rough-in/inspection, finish, closeout. That’s more usable than a general list.

turns.agent_response:
Use a **phase checklist with one owner and hard hold points**.

## 1) Offer / due diligence
Verify:
- title status
- permit status
- open code issues
- utility shutoff / restart requirements
- tenant access constraints
- scope assumptions
- long-lead items
- change-order policy

## 2) Pre-close
Confirm:
- title commitment / exceptions
- payoff demands
- lien risks
- utility transfer plan
- insurance effective date
- contractor availability
- inspection dates
- final scope sheet signed off

## 3) First 30 days
Lock in:
- demo
- discovery items
- long-lead orders
- rough schedule
- inspection windows
- weekly update cadence

## 4) Rough-in / inspection
Track:
- rough plumbing
- rough electrical
- rough HVAC
- framing / structural fixes
- rough inspections
- correction items
- re-inspection dates

**Hold point:** no drywall or finish work until roughs pass.

## 5) Finish
Track:
- drywall / paint
- flooring
- cabinets
- trim / doors
- fixtures
- appliances
- punch list

**Hard rule:** no verbal change orders.  
If it changes scope or finish date, it must be:
- written
- priced
- schedule-adjusted
- approved before work starts

## 6) Closeout
Confirm:
- final inspection
- lien waivers
- warranties
- final invoices
- photo set
- permit finals
- punch list closed
- retainage release conditions met

## Operating rules
- **One owner for the log**
- **One master scope sheet**
- **One schedule with dependencies**
- **No payment without hold-point completion**
- **No assumption without documentation**

## Fastest way to keep it from drifting
Use a weekly control review:
- done
- blocked
- late
- waiting on inspection
- waiting on material
- waiting on paperwork

If you want, I can turn this into a **copy-paste checklist by phase** with checkbox items and sign-off lines.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. Still too clean and a little abstract.

I want the checklist itself, not another framework around the checklist. Put it in a format I can actually use on a job — checkbox, owner, due date, status, hold point. And don’t leave out the ugly stuff:

- utility account transfer / shutoff confirmation
- permit card posted and current
- code violations logged before close
- contractor insurance and license verified
- lender draw requirements if there’s financing
- material lead times before demo starts
- change order signoff before work proceeds
- lien waiver before each payment, not just at the end
- final cleaning / trash haul / lock change / keys / alarm codes at closeout

Also, I’d want a separate line for who owns each phase. One person. Not “team.”

If you can do it, make it a one-page working checklist by phase with a short pre-close section and a tighter closeout section. That’s the part that usually gets sloppy.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. Here’s a **working one-page rehab checklist by phase** you can actually use.

---

# Rehab Control Checklist

**Project:** ____________________  
**Address:** ____________________  
**Owner/PM:** ____________________  
**GC:** ____________________  
**Lender:** ____________________

## Phase 1: Pre-Close / Due Diligence
| Check | Owner | Due Date | Status | Hold Point |
|---|---|---:|---|---|
| Title commitment reviewed |  |  |  |  |
| Exceptions / liens / judgments logged |  |  |  |  |
| Permit status verified |  |  |  |  |
| Open code violations logged |  |  |  |  |
| Utility accounts / shutoff / restart plan confirmed |  |  |  |  |
| Contractor license verified |  |  |  |  |
| Contractor insurance verified |  |  |  |  |
| Scope sheet reviewed and signed |  |  |  |  |
| Long-lead materials identified |  |  |  |  |
| Material lead times confirmed |  |  |  |  |
| Financing draw requirements confirmed |  |  |  |  |
| Change-order policy confirmed in writing |  |  |  |  |

**Pre-close hold point:** no closing until title, permits, utilities, and financing conditions are clear.

---

## Phase 2: Mobilization / Start
| Check | Owner | Due Date | Status | Hold Point |
|---|---|---:|---|---|
| One person assigned to own log |  |  |  |  |
| Master scope sheet issued |  |  |  |  |
| Work schedule with dependencies issued |  |  |  |  |
| Demo start date confirmed |  |  |  |  |
| Long-lead materials ordered |  |  |  |  |
| Access plan confirmed |  |  |  |  |
| Utility shutoff/restart date confirmed |  |  |  |  |
| Permit card posted and current |  |  |  |  |

**Hold point:** no demo until materials, utilities, and permits are lined up.

---

## Phase 3: Demo / Discovery
| Check | Owner | Due Date | Status | Hold Point |
|---|---|---:|---|---|
| Demo completed |  |  |  |  |
| Hidden conditions documented |  |  |  |  |
| Water damage / rot / mold logged |  |  |  |  |
| Unpermitted work identified |  |  |  |  |
| Scope changes priced in writing |  |  |  |  |
| Change orders approved before work proceeds |  |  |  |  |

**Hold point:** no rough-in changes without written approval.

---

## Phase 4: Rough-In / Inspections
| Check | Owner | Due Date | Status | Hold Point |
|---|---|---:|---|---|
| Rough plumbing complete |  |  |  |  |
| Rough electrical complete |  |  |  |  |
| Rough HVAC complete |  |  |  |  |
| Framing/structural corrections complete |  |  |  |  |
| Rough inspections scheduled |  |  |  |  |
| Rough inspections passed |  |  |  |  |
| Re-inspections passed, if needed |  |  |  |  |

**Hold point:** no drywall, insulation, or finish work until roughs pass.

---

## Phase 5: Finish Work
| Check | Owner | Due Date | Status | Hold Point |
|---|---|---:|---|---|
| Insulation complete |  |  |  |  |
| Drywall complete |  |  |  |  |
| Paint complete |  |  |  |  |
| Flooring complete |  |  |  |  |
| Cabinets / trim / doors complete |  |  |  |  |
| Fixtures / appliances installed |  |  |  |  |
| Change orders signed before any added work |  |  |  |  |
| Lien waiver collected before each payment |  |  |  |  |

**Hold point:** no payment without documented progress and lien waiver.

---

## Phase 6: Closeout
| Check | Owner | Due Date | Status | Hold Point |
|---|---|---:|---|---|
| Final cleaning complete |  |  |  |  |
| Trash hauled off |  |  |  |  |
| Punch list complete |  |  |  |  |
| Final inspection passed |  |  |  |  |
| Permit final closed |  |  |  |  |
| Final lien waivers in hand |  |  |  |  |
| Final invoice received |  |  |  |  |
| Warranty docs received |  |  |  |  |
| Keys delivered |  |  |  |  |
| Lock change complete |  |  |  |  |
| Alarm codes changed / confirmed |  |  |  |  |
| Utility accounts transferred |  |  |  |  |

**Closeout hold point:** no final payment until punch list, final inspection, lien waivers, and closeout docs are complete.

---

## Hard rules
- **One owner per phase**
- **No verbal change orders**
- **No payment without lien waiver**
- **No finish work before rough approval**
- **No closeout without final inspection**

If you want, I can turn this into a **clean spreadsheet-style version** with blank lines you can paste into Excel or Google Sheets.

user_simulator.text:
This is usable. Finally.

It’s not perfect, but it’s close enough to work with. The phase split is right, and the ugly stuff is in there where it belongs. One owner per phase, hold points, lien waivers before each payment — that’s the part people usually half-do and then wonder why the job drifts.

I’d still tighten two things when I put it in my own sheet:
- add a line for **permit card photographed and filed** at start, not just posted
- add **who has the keys / alarm / utility login** in closeout, not just that they were changed

But yeah. This is the actual checklist structure I was after, not another lecture about project control.
